name: Avenc de l'Infern
elevation_change: Not relevant; only a small slope near the pit
coordinates_utm: DATUM EUROPEAN 1950: 462620 E, 4380700 N. DATUM ETRS89: 462526 E, 4380495 N
equipment: Several short ropes, some carabiners and slings
description: At -132 m, this is the deepest known cave in the Serra de Na Burguesa. Despite requiring vertical progression equipment, it lacks major vertical drops and reaches its lowest point in a rock-filled chamber containing the most beautiful formations and ancient paleolevel marks. The cave is filled with loose rocks throughout, making it one of the most dangerous cavities in the range alongside Avenc des Pastor.
full_description:
Description of the cavity

The description I am going to give of this cavity corresponds to the Vía dets Antics (Route of the Ancients) since it is the only one I have done of the two possible routes.

Once in the Sala d'Entrada (Entrance Hall), we will head to the deepest area of it where two old pitons are installed that can be used (at least as of 2010) to descend through a small hole that gives continuity to the descent.

This cavity, at -132 m, is the deepest known to date in the Serra de Na Burguesa. However, despite the fact that the use of vertical progression equipment is necessary, it lacks major vertical drops, and the deepest zone is reached in a chamber where a great quantity of rocks accumulates. In the deepest zone of the chamber are found the most beautiful formations of the chasm, where ancient paleolevels can also be contemplated.

One of the characteristics of this cavity, and perhaps where its name comes from, is the great quantity of rocks it harbors in its interior, which are a danger to be taken into account. Together with the Avenc des Pastor, it is one of the most dangerous cavities of the Serra de Na Burguesa.

Note: If you wish to visit the chasm via the new route, which does not require a rope, you can do so from the Sala d'Entrada. You can obtain all the information in ENDINS 21, where you will also find the survey.
